title: Best Grinder for Espresso: Comparing All Four Arco Grinders
hero_image_alt: Four Arco grinders arranged in a diagonal line on a dark slate surface, the Filtro on the far left followed by the Preciso, Macinino, and Zero, each with a small pile of freshly ground coffee in front of it showing slightly different textures, dramatic side lighting from the right creating long shadows and emphasizing the metallic finishes, shot from a low angle to give the grinders a commanding presence
intro: Your grinder is the most important piece of equipment in your espresso setup. More than the machine, more than the water, more than the beans — though all of those matter — the grinder determines whether your shot will be balanced and sweet or muddled and bitter. A good grinder produces uniform particles that extract evenly. A poor grinder produces a chaotic mix of fine dust and coarse boulders that makes consistent espresso nearly impossible. Arco makes four grinders: the Filtro at $349, the Preciso at $449, the Macinino at $549, and the Zero at $699. All four are burr grinders built for precision, but they differ in burr geometry, adjustment mechanism, and intended use case. Here is how they compare specifically for espresso.

body.heading: Conical vs Flat Burrs: What the Geometry Means for Your Cup
body.content: The most fundamental difference between grinders is burr geometry. Conical burrs consist of a cone-shaped inner burr spinning inside a ring-shaped outer burr. Coffee beans are fed in from the top and crushed between the two surfaces as they travel downward. The Arco Preciso and Filtro both use conical burrs. Flat burrs consist of two parallel, horizontally oriented discs with teeth that face each other. Beans enter through the center and are ground outward to the edges. The Arco Macinino and Zero both use flat burrs. The practical difference comes down to flavor profile and particle distribution. Conical burrs tend to produce a slightly bimodal particle distribution — a main peak of target-sized particles with a smaller population of fines. In espresso, this bimodal distribution creates a cup with more body, a richer mouthfeel, and a flavor profile that many people describe as more traditional or Italian in character. The fines contribute to the body and thickness of the shot. Flat burrs produce a more unimodal, or uniform, particle distribution. This means more particles are the same size, and fewer outliers exist at the fine or coarse end. The result in the cup is typically more clarity, more distinct flavor notes, and a cleaner finish. Light-roast single-origin coffees, which have complex and delicate flavor compounds, often shine with flat burrs because those flavors are not masked by body from excessive fines. Neither geometry is objectively better. The choice depends on what you value in your espresso. If you prefer rich, full-bodied shots with deep chocolate and caramel notes, conical burrs will likely appeal to you. If you prefer bright, clean shots where you can taste the specific origin character of the coffee, flat burrs are the way to go. Many serious home baristas eventually own one of each.

body.heading: The Filtro and Preciso: Conical Burr Options
body.content: The Arco Filtro at $349 is designed primarily for filter coffee — pour-over, drip, French press — but it can grind fine enough for espresso. Its conical burrs are 40mm in diameter, and its stepped adjustment mechanism covers a wide range from very coarse to espresso fine. If you brew both filter coffee and espresso, the Filtro offers versatility at the lowest price point. However, the stepped adjustment means you may find that the optimal espresso setting falls between two steps, forcing a small compromise in either direction. For many coffees this is barely noticeable, but as your palate develops, you may find it limiting. The Arco Preciso at $449 is purpose-built for espresso. Its 48mm conical burrs are larger than the Filtro's, which means they grind faster and run cooler — less heat transferred to the coffee during grinding, preserving volatile aromatics. The critical difference is the Preciso's stepless adjustment mechanism. Instead of clicking between fixed positions, you turn the collar smoothly through an infinite range. This lets you make micro-adjustments so small that the difference between settings is a matter of seconds in shot time. When you are dialing in a new bag and the shot runs at 23 seconds on one setting and 28 seconds on the next, a stepless grinder lets you land precisely on 25 seconds. That precision is what separates a good shot from a great one. The Preciso also features a low-retention design that minimizes the amount of ground coffee left inside the grinder between uses. Retention matters because stale grounds from your previous session mix with fresh grounds from the current one, diluting flavor. The Preciso retains less than 0.5 grams, which means your first shot of the day tastes as fresh as your last.

body.heading: The Macinino and Zero: Flat Burr Options
body.content: The Arco Macinino at $549 is a flat burr grinder designed specifically for espresso, and it is the choice for anyone who prioritizes clarity and flavor separation in their cup. Its 58mm flat burrs produce the kind of uniform particle distribution that makes light-roast single-origin espresso sing. You will taste the blueberry in an Ethiopian, the citrus in a Kenyan, the stone fruit in a Colombian — not buried under body, but presented clearly and distinctly. The Macinino uses a stepless adjustment system with a micrometer-style dial that gives you extraordinarily fine control over grind size. The motor is deliberately slow — around 400 RPM — to minimize heat generation during grinding. Heat is the enemy of flavor in freshly ground coffee, and the Macinino's low-speed approach preserves every volatile compound that makes freshly roasted coffee smell and taste extraordinary. The grind chamber is designed for minimal retention, holding back less than a gram between uses. The Arco Zero at $699 is the flagship grinder in the lineup and is built around the single-dose workflow. Single-dosing means you weigh out exactly the amount of whole beans you need for one shot — typically 18 to 20 grams — and grind them all at once, rather than keeping beans in a hopper and grinding on demand. This approach eliminates retention entirely because there are no leftover beans or grounds in the system. Every particle that goes into your portafilter is freshly ground from the dose you just weighed. The Zero's 64mm flat burrs are the largest in the Arco lineup, producing exceptional particle uniformity at espresso settings. The bellows on top of the grinder push any remaining grounds out of the chamber after grinding, ensuring true zero retention. The adjustment is stepless with numeric markings for repeatable settings. If you switch between multiple coffees — a blend for milk drinks in the morning and a single-origin for afternoon espresso — the Zero lets you do so without wasting coffee on purging or dialing in.

body.heading: Which Grinder Should You Buy for Espresso
body.content: If you are on a tight budget and also brew filter coffee, the Filtro at $349 is a capable starting point that will grow with you. Its espresso performance is good, though not as precise as the dedicated espresso grinders. You may eventually want to upgrade, but the Filtro will get you making real espresso at the lowest entry cost. If espresso is your primary focus and you prefer traditional, full-bodied shots — the kind of thick, syrupy espresso that Italian cafes are known for — the Preciso at $449 is the ideal choice. Its conical burrs and stepless adjustment are designed for exactly this style, and its low retention means consistently fresh grinding. If you are drawn to lighter roasts, single-origin coffees, and the specialty coffee movement's emphasis on clarity and distinct flavor notes, the Macinino at $549 is your grinder. Its flat burrs and slow motor are engineered to bring out the best in these coffees. If you are the kind of person who weighs every dose, switches between beans regularly, and wants absolute zero retention, the Zero at $699 is the definitive choice. Its single-dose design, large flat burrs, and bellows system mean that what you weigh is what you grind and what you taste — nothing more, nothing less. One final consideration is noise. Flat burr grinders typically run louder than conical burr grinders because the motor works harder to spin larger, heavier burrs. If you make espresso early in the morning while others are sleeping, the Preciso's conical burrs are noticeably quieter than the Macinino or Zero. It is a practical detail that matters more than most reviews acknowledge.

key_takeaways: Conical burrs (Filtro, Preciso) produce rich, full-bodied espresso; flat burrs (Macinino, Zero) produce cleaner, more flavor-distinct shots.
The Filtro ($349) is versatile for filter and espresso but has stepped adjustment that limits precision.
The Preciso ($449) is the best conical option for espresso with stepless adjustment and under 0.5g retention.
The Macinino ($549) is the flat burr choice for clarity-focused espresso with a slow, heat-reducing motor.
The Zero ($699) is built for single-dose workflow with zero retention and the largest 64mm flat burrs in the lineup.

seo.title: Best Grinder for Espresso: Filtro vs Preciso vs Macinino vs Zero | Arco
seo.meta_description: Compare all four Arco grinders for espresso. Understand conical vs flat burrs, stepped vs stepless adjustment, and single-dose workflow to find your perfect match.
